s: Former X-rated cinema turned cult film and cocktail den
w: A 1970s adult cinema repurposed into one of Madrid's most atmospheric film spaces. The main screen survives inside a crumbling Lavapiés palacio, and the bar operates in what was the foyer where patrons once queued for something else entirely. Cult screenings, midnight slots, the original projection booth still working. The seedier the past, the better the vibe.
_facts: Built in a historic palacio on Calle del Duque de Alba 4, Lavapiés
Operated as an X-rated cinema (sala X) before rebranding as an arthouse / cocktail venue
Interior retains original cinema seating and projection setup
Named for the X-certificate (sala equis = X cinema) that once designated it

s: Spain's national film archive, still screening in a 1923 Art Nouveau sala
w: Home of the Filmoteca Española — Spain's Ministry of Culture-backed film archive — and still the most atmospheric cinema in Madrid. The 1923 building is a protected monument; tickets cost a few coins and the programme is aggressively non-commercial. Single screens, no adverts, an educated hush. An outlier that has survived everything.
_facts: Built in 1923 in Art Nouveau style; declared Bien de Interés Cultural
Houses the Filmoteca Española, charged with restoring and conserving Spain's film heritage
Part of the Institute of Cinematography and Audiovisual Arts (ICAA)
Located at Calle de Santa Isabel 3, Lavapiés

n: Ermita de San Antonio de la Florida
s: Goya painted the ceiling here and is buried under it
w: In 1798 Goya spent four months painting the dome and ceiling of this small royal chapel near the Manzanares river. He is buried beneath the very frescoes he made — though his skull was missing when the body was transferred in 1919, and has never been recovered. The chapel is now a museum; a replica next door handles services. The extraordinary ceiling is his.
_facts: Neoclassical chapel built 1792–1798
Goya's ceiling and dome frescoes painted 1798
Goya's remains were transferred here from Bordeaux in 1919, but the skull was absent and has never been found
A second identical chapel was built next door for religious services so the original could serve as a museum

s: Royal tapestry workshop founded in 1720, still weaving by hand
w: Founded under Philip V in 1720, the Royal Tapestry Factory has operated without interruption for over 300 years. Weavers still work at wooden looms that were old when Goya supplied cartoons for them in the 1770s. The building is on Calle Fuenterrabía in Retiro; visitors can watch threads being set by eye. Not a museum — an active workshop, one of Europe's last.
_facts: Founded 1720 under Philip V as part of the Bourbon reform of Spanish manufacturing
Goya produced around 63 tapestry cartoons for the factory between 1775 and 1792
Still in active production using traditional hand-weaving methods on historical looms
Factory building dates from the 18th century; located at Calle de Fuenterrabía 2

s: Iron-and-tile exhibition pavilion inside Retiro park, now a Reina Sofía annexe
w: Built for the 1883 National Mining and Arts Exhibition by Ricardo Velázquez Bosco, who gave the palace its name. Brick, iron and ceramic tiles in a style somewhere between Beaux-Arts and something purely Spanish — the tilework was made in Valencia. The Reina Sofía uses it for large-scale temporary shows; no permanent collection. One of the park's best secrets.
_facts: Built in 1883 for the National Mining and Arts Exhibition by architect Ricardo Velázquez Bosco
Notable ceramic and iron facade; designed to be temporary but became permanent
Located inside Buen Retiro Park
Now an exhibition hall run by the Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofía

s: The old city abattoir, reborn as Madrid's biggest cultural laboratory
w: For half a century from 1925, every animal slaughtered for Madrid's tables passed through this 165,000-square-metre complex on the banks of the Manzanares. The neo-Mudéjar brick warehouses were shuttered in 1979. Today they house cinema, theatre, design studios, a market and one of the city's best street-art programmes. The hooks are gone but the scale hasn't changed.
_facts: Built 1908-1928 as El Matadero y Mercado Municipal de Ganados (slaughterhouse and livestock market)
Closed in 1979; reopened as contemporary arts centre in 2006-2011 in phases
165,000 square metres of neo-Mudéjar brick buildings in the Arganzuela district
Run by Madrid City Council; programming includes cinema, theatre, design and visual arts

s: Madrid's great tertulia café, unchanged since 1888
w: Open since 1888, the Gijón was where the city's writers and artists argued in the 20th century — Valle-Inclán, Ortega y Gasset, Hemingway among the regulars. Marble tables, dark wood, a boulevard terrace on the Paseo de Recoletos. The literary prize it runs annually is taken seriously. Little has changed and that is precisely the point.
_facts: Founded in 1888 on Paseo de Recoletos 21
Famous as a tertulias venue — informal intellectual salons — throughout the 20th century
Regulars said to have included Ramón del Valle-Inclán, José Ortega y Gasset and Ernest Hemingway
Runs the annual Premio Café Gijón literary prize since 1949

s: Madrid's finest Art Nouveau facade — read it like a stone forest
w: Designed in 1902 by José Grases Riera for financier Francisco Longoria, this is the city's most extravagant modernista building — stone tendrils, lily-pad balconies, a roofline that looks grown rather than built. Now the headquarters of the authors' rights society SGAE; viewable only from the street. The building opposite Calle Fernando VI is the correct angle.
_facts: Designed 1902 by architect José Grases Riera for Francisco Javier González Longoria
Considered Madrid's finest example of Art Nouveau / modernista architecture
Now the headquarters of the SGAE (Sociedad General de Autores y Editores)
Located on Calle Fernando VI in the Chueca district

s: Haunted palace at the Cibeles roundabout, seat of the Casa de América
w: Built for the Marquis of Linares in the 1870s, this Italianate palace at the Cibeles roundabout spent much of the 20th century empty after its last owners died childless. Madrileños claimed it was haunted by the ghost of a daughter born of an incestuous union; a 1990s TV broadcast purported to record the ghost. Declared a national monument in 1976, it now houses the Casa de América.
_facts: Built 1872-1900 for José de Murga y Reolid, First Marquis of Linares
Declared national historic-artistic monument in 1976
Famous haunting legend involving the ghost of 'Rosarito', said to be the product of an incestuous marriage between the Marquis and a half-sister
A 1990 televised ghost-hunt by journalist Eugenio Sánchez Pascual sparked renewed public interest
Now serves as the seat of the Casa de América cultural organisation

s: A Gilded Age collector's obsession, now open to the public on Serrano
w: José Lázaro Galdiano was a publisher and pathological collector who amassed 12,600 objects — mediaeval ivories, Bosch paintings, Flemish clocks, Goya portraits, Leonardo drawings — and bequeathed them all, along with his Salamanca mansion, to the Spanish state. Opened as a museum in 1951. It remains wildly uneven, intensely personal and unlike anything else in Madrid.
_facts: José Lázaro Galdiano (1862-1947) was a publisher who assembled a collection of 12,600 objects
Collection spans mediaeval art through 19th century; includes works attributed to Bosch, El Greco, Velázquez, Goya and Leonardo da Vinci
Galdiano bequeathed his home (Parque Florido villa, Calle Serrano 122) and entire collection to the Spanish state
Museum opened 27 January 1951

s: Habsburg-era prison that became a palace and now hosts the foreign ministry
w: Built as the Cárcel de Corte (Court Prison) in the 1630s under Philip IV, this is one of the earliest examples of Baroque civil architecture in Madrid. Its towers and slate spires look more like a palace than a gaol, which eventually it became. The Foreign Ministry has occupied it since 1931. The arcaded courtyard can be glimpsed through the gate.
_facts: Built 1629-1636 as the Cárcel de Corte (Court Prison) under Philip IV
One of the earliest Baroque civic buildings in Madrid, designed by Juan Gómez de Mora
Converted to the Juzgados (Courts) and later the Foreign Ministry in 1931
Declared Bien de Interés Cultural; a well-preserved example of Habsburg-period Madrid

s: Madrid's oldest square, once the straw market of the medieval city
w: Before the Plaza Mayor existed, the Plaza de la Paja was where the city traded. Straw and hay for the horses of the court; later the home of the Bishop's palace and the Capilla del Obispo, whose Gothic-Renaissance chapel dates from the 1530s. Now a quiet, sloped garden, traffic-free, with some of the best tapas bars in La Latina below it on the terraces.
_facts: One of the oldest squares in Madrid, predating the Plaza Mayor by centuries
Name means 'Straw Square' — a medieval straw and hay market serving the royal household
Flanked by the Capilla del Obispo (Bishop's Chapel), begun in the 1520s, one of the best Gothic-Renaissance buildings in Madrid
Now a pedestrianised garden square in the La Latina neighbourhood

s: The best jazz club on the Iberian Peninsula, by some distance
w: Since 1982, a corner of Plaza del Ángel has been the centre of Madrid's jazz scene. Voted one of the world's great jazz clubs by DownBeat magazine; artists who played the Blue Note in New York have appeared here the same month. Deco interior, low light, no bad seats. The queue forms early and does not move.
_facts: Opened 1982 at Plaza del Ángel 10, Barrio de las Letras
Listed among the world's great jazz clubs by DownBeat magazine
Hosts international jazz artists who perform regularly at marquee venues in New York and Europe
Art Deco interior with intimate seating capacity of around 150

s: Madrid's arts club rooftop is the best vantage point in the city centre
w: Founded in 1880, the Círculo is a private cultural club — theatre, exhibitions, a ballroom, a cafe — housed in a 1926 building on Calle Alcalá whose rooftop terrace is the reason most people go. The Goddess Minerva on the parapet holds a flame, not a lamp. Declared Bien de Interés Cultural in 1981. Membership costs less than most gym subscriptions.
_facts: Founded 1880; current building completed 1926 by architect Antonio Palacios
Declared Bien de Interés Cultural in 1981
The rooftop mirador offers panoramic views over central Madrid; accessible for a small fee
Features a theatre, cinemas, exhibition halls, a famous cafe and a ballroom

s: Former royal tobacco factory taken over by artists and never given back
w: Built in 1792 as the Real Fábrica de Tabaco, this vast block on Embajadores once rolled the cigarettes of Spain. After the tobacco company departed, artists moved in; the Ministry of Culture eventually made it official. Now a self-managed cultural space covering two hectares — street art, concerts, theatre, a boxing gym, a community garden. Free, always open, never finished.
_facts: Built in 1792 as a royal tobacco factory (Real Fábrica de Tabaco)
Located on Calle de Embajadores 53, Lavapiés
Became a cultural squat after closure; officially co-managed by the Ministry of Culture from 2010
Self-managed two-hectare space hosting street art, performance, community events

s: The world's most prestigious bullring, in neo-Mudéjar brick
w: Inaugurated 1931 and still the cathedral of tauromachy: 23,798 seats, a capacity that makes it the largest bullring in Spain. The season runs March to October; the San Isidro feria in May is when reputations are made or broken. The neo-Mudéjar facade of brick and azulejo tile is best appreciated from outside, where the scale is harder to deny.
_facts: Inaugurated June 17, 1931 in the Guindalera quarter, Salamanca district
Capacity of 23,798, making it the largest bullring in Spain
Now considered the second-largest bullring still operating for its original purpose worldwide
The San Isidro feria held each May is considered the most prestigious bullfighting event in the world

s: Madrid's great Organicist tower — concrete grown in rings like a tree
w: Designed in 1961 by Francisco Javier Sáenz de Oiza and completed 1969, Torres Blancas is the defining statement of Spanish Organicism: a single circular residential tower whose balconies erupt in cylindrical pods, the whole thing resembling a living organism rather than a building. Commissioned by industrialist Juan Huarte, who also backed Chillida and Oteiza. Still residential.
_facts: Designed 1961 by Francisco Javier Sáenz de Oiza; completed 1969
Commissioned by industrialist Juan Huarte, patron of the Basque avant-garde
Noted example of Spanish Organicism — an architecture that treats building as biological growth
Located on Avenida de América 37, Chamartín district
Still a residential building

s: Triumphal arch begun under Napoleon, finished to celebrate his defeat
w: Construction started in 1812 on the orders of Joseph Bonaparte, who wanted a triumphal arch on the Toledo road. French troops left before it was finished. The arch was eventually completed in 1827 — not to celebrate Napoleon but to welcome Ferdinand VII back from exile. The gate survived and the irony was total. Declared Bien de Interés Cultural in 1996.
_facts: Construction began 1812 under Joseph Bonaparte (Joseph I of Spain)
Not completed until 1827, after the Napoleonic Wars ended
Ultimately dedicated to Fernando VII rather than Napoleon or Joseph
Declared Bien de Interés Cultural in 1996
Neo-classical design; one of two surviving old city gates in Madrid along with the Puerta de Alcalá

s: Churrigueresque baroque portal, then Madrid's history from Hapsburg to Republic
w: The portal alone is worth a detour: a 1722 churrigueresque facade of stone lace by Pedro de Ribera, considered one of the finest examples of Spanish Baroque in the city. Behind it, a former hospice now holds an exhaustive and undervisited chronicle of Madrid — city maps, royal portraits, a miniature model of 1830 Madrid that took 30 years to build. Opened as Museo Municipal in 1929.
_facts: Building is the former Hospicio de San Fernando, 18th century
Baroque portal by Pedro de Ribera, completed 1722, considered a masterwork of Churrigueresque style
Opened as Museo Municipal in 1929; renamed Museo de Historia de Madrid in 2007
Houses a famous 1:500 scale model of 1830 Madrid by León Gil de Palacio, which took 30 years to complete
Located at Calle Fuencarral 78

s: Eight hectares of living science next to the Prado, founded by Carlos III
w: Moved to its present site in 1781 under Carlos III — the Enlightenment king who reshaped Madrid's Paseo del Prado into a boulevard of science. Eight hectares holding 90,000 plants and a herbarium of five million specimens. The three terraces (French formal, economic plants, trees) reflect 18th-century classification systems. Entrance through the Puerta de Murillo.
_facts: Founded 1755; moved to current location on Paseo del Prado in 1781 under Carlos III
8 hectares holding approximately 90,000 plants from around the world
Herbarium contains approximately five million specimens
Designed in Neoclassical style by Francesco Sabatini and Juan de Villanueva
Attached to the Spanish National Research Council (CSIC)

s: The first map to show America, plus four centuries of Spanish sea power
w: Inside the Navy Ministry on the Paseo del Prado lies one of Madrid's most surprising museums: a history of the Spanish Navy from the Catholic Monarchs to the present. The star exhibit is the Juan de la Cosa map of 1500 — the earliest known cartographic representation of the Americas. Galleon models, astrolabes, the log of Columbus's second voyage. Often empty.
_facts: Houses the Juan de la Cosa nautical chart of 1500, the oldest known map to show the New World
Collection spans 15th century to present, documenting the Spanish Navy
Part of the National Museums of Spain; attached to the Ministry of Defence
Located at Paseo del Prado 5 inside the Navy Ministry building

s: The post office that Madrileños called 'Our Lady of Communications'
w: Completed in 1919 as the Palacio de Comunicaciones, this white Cathedral of Post was designed by Antonio Palacios and Joaquín Otamendi and became so grand that Madrileños nicknamed it 'Nuestra Señora de las Comunicaciones'. The city's main telegraph, telephone and postal hub until 2009, it is now Madrid's city hall. The CentroCentro gallery and the mirador on the ninth floor are open to the public.
_facts: Built 1907-1919 by architects Antonio Palacios and Joaquín Otamendi
Served as Madrid's central post office and telecommunications hub until 2009
Nicknamed 'Nuestra Señora de las Comunicaciones' by locals because of its cathedral-like scale
Became Madrid City Hall (Ayuntamiento) in 2011; public cultural centre CentroCentro opened same year
Rooftop mirador open to the public

s: A sherry-only bar where the rules haven't changed since the Civil War
w: On Calle Echegaray since the 1930s, La Venencia serves only sherry — manzanilla, fino, amontillado, oloroso — drawn from barrels and poured into small glasses. No photographs. No mobile phones (the signs say so). The barmen write the tally in chalk on the bar. The walls are the colour of tobacco. Republican posters from the Civil War still decorate the back room.
_facts: Operating on Calle de Echegaray 7 since the 1930s
Serves only sherry (Jerez) poured straight from barrels — no beer, wine or spirits
Famous no-photograph and no-mobile-phone policy maintained by staff
Walls and memorabilia reflect the bar's Civil War-era Republican sympathies
Chalk tallying system on the bar is a surviving tradition

s: Lavapiés neighbourhood market with African, Asian and local traders side by side
w: Built in the 1940s in the workingclass Lavapiés quarter, this municipal market evolved differently from Madrid's gentrified mercados: affordable, community-run, mixed. Halal butchers next to pintxos bars, Bengali grocery sellers alongside vermouth stands. On Sundays it overflows onto Calle Tribulete. The haggling, the noise and the genuine mix are the point.
_facts: Municipal covered market on Calle de Embajadores 41, built in the 1940s
Located in Lavapiés, one of Madrid's most ethnically diverse neighbourhoods
Mix of local food stalls, ethnic food vendors, bars and social spaces
Managed by the community with city council oversight; resistant to full gentrification

s: 300 years of Spanish fashion — from court mantillas to Balenciaga
w: Spain's national fashion and costume museum holds 70,000 garments and textiles spanning five centuries. Court dress, rural costume, 19th-century bourgeois fashion and 20th-century Spanish couture including Balenciaga and Pertegaz. The building itself — a 1966 Alejandro de la Sota structure at the edge of the Moncloa campus — is quietly important. Underpacked, undersung.
_facts: National museum devoted to the history of clothing and fashion in Spain
Collection of approximately 70,000 garments, accessories and textile pieces
Includes court dress, regional costumes, and 20th-century couture by Spanish designers including Balenciaga and Pertegaz
Located on Avenida Juan de Herrera 2, Ciudad Universitaria campus
One of Spain's National Museums, attached to the Ministry of Culture
